scenarios.title: The Disciplined Builder
scenarios.description: A startup that prioritises stability, quality, and timing over raw speed. Every decision compounds trust — and trust becomes the moat.

steps.title: Fix Bugs First
steps.context: The team resists investor pressure to ship new features and spends the quarter on stability.
steps.market_impact: Early adopters stop churning. Word-of-mouth begins. Trust is the moat at this stage — one bad review can kill a SaaS in a niche market.

steps.title: Hire 1 Engineer
steps.context: Velocity is stable, morale is high, and the team can absorb a new hire without chaos.
steps.market_impact: Feature delivery accelerates. Competitors are shipping faster but the product's quality reputation starts differentiating it in the market.

steps.title: Release Carefully
steps.context: Despite pressure to move fast, the team maintains quality gates before each release.
steps.market_impact: First enterprise prospect appears. Enterprise buyers do due diligence — system stability is a hard requirement. A single outage here loses a 6-month deal.

steps.title: Hire Sales
steps.context: Product is stable enough to confidently sell. The team finally has something worth putting in front of buyers.
steps.market_impact: MRR grows 3x in 90 days. The market was waiting — the bottleneck was never the product, it was distribution.

steps.title: Invest in Tech Debt
steps.context: Before scaling further, the team cleans the foundation to handle the load of growth ahead.
steps.market_impact: No visible user impact now, but engineering velocity jumps the following quarter. Investors notice deployment frequency as a proxy for team health.

steps.title: Build New Feature
steps.context: On a solid, clean base, the team ships a high-demand feature quickly and confidently.
steps.market_impact: Feature goes viral in the niche community. Competitor users start migrating. This is the compounding return of doing the boring work in Q1.

steps.title: Hire Sales Again
steps.context: Growing revenue justifies expanding the sales team to capture the expanding pipeline.
steps.market_impact: Pipeline doubles. The company enters Series A conversations. Revenue growth is now predictable — the best signal for institutional investors.

steps.title: Release Fast
steps.context: With a larger, stable team and clean codebase, shipping speed is now sustainable without sacrificing quality.
steps.market_impact: Shipping velocity becomes a brand identity. 'They ship fast and it works' is the reputation. Series A closes.

scenarios.title: Growth at All Costs
scenarios.description: A startup that chases metrics over fundamentals. Every shortcut feels like speed — until the compounding debt calls in all at once.

steps.title: Ship Features, Fix Later
steps.context: Pressure from investors to show growth. The team skips bug triage and pushes the next feature.
steps.market_impact: Launch gets attention. But 20% of new signups hit the same 3 bugs and churn within 2 weeks. Customer acquisition cost is being thrown away.

steps.title: Release Fast
steps.context: Double down on shipping speed. Daily deploys with no quality gates.
steps.market_impact: Bug rate compounds. A production incident takes the product down for 4 hours. The first negative review appears and will rank on Google for years.

steps.title: Hire Sales Too Early
steps.context: Try to sell an unstable product. Sales reps are promising features that don't reliably work.
steps.market_impact: High pipeline, very low close rate. Churn from closed deals is 60% within 90 days — poisoning the reference pool and destroying future word-of-mouth.

steps.title: Ignore Tech Debt
steps.context: Sprint to hit end-of-year metrics. Leadership defers the debt conversation to January.
steps.market_impact: Burn rate is high. MRR is not growing proportionally. Runway drops below 9 months. Investors ask hard questions at the board meeting.

steps.title: Another New Feature
steps.context: Instead of addressing stability, the team bets on a new feature to re-spark growth.
steps.market_impact: Feature ships with new bugs on a broken foundation. The core user base — who stayed despite problems — starts leaving. This is the tipping point.

steps.title: Panic Hire an Engineer
steps.context: Emergency hire to tackle the mounting bug backlog, but the timing is reactive not strategic.
steps.market_impact: Coordination cost spikes. New engineer spends 6 weeks understanding the codebase. Velocity drops before it rises. Existing team morale collapses.

steps.title: Invest in Tech Debt (Too Late)
steps.context: Finally forced to address stability, but the team's velocity stalls completely for weeks.
steps.market_impact: Nothing ships for 8 weeks. A competitor fills the gap. The sales pipeline goes cold. An enterprise prospect walks away after waiting too long.

steps.title: Desperate Push to Ship
steps.context: With runway nearly exhausted, the team pushes releases to show investor activity.
steps.market_impact: Burn rate never recovered. Bridge round fails. The product had real value — the market wanted it — but operational decisions burned the runway before product-market fit could compound.
